title: Pimientos de Padrón
subtitle: Blistered Spanish green peppers with sea salt
excerpt: Pimientos de Padrón are small green Spanish peppers fried in very hot olive oil until blistered and slightly charred, then scattered with sea salt. They take five minutes to cook. Most are mild, but one or two in a batch are fiery hot; this unpredictability is part of the appeal.
sourceNotes: Pimientos de Padrón take their name from the town of Padrón in Galicia, northwest Spain, where the peppers were first cultivated. They were introduced to Galicia from South America by Franciscan friars in the sixteenth century. The dish is now eaten across Spain as a tapa.

content.text: Pimientos de Padrón are small green peppers fried quickly in very hot olive oil until blistered and dark at the tips, then scattered with coarse salt. Most are mild and sweet, but roughly one in ten is unexpectedly hot. They cook in five minutes and are one of the most satisfying things you can put on a table at the start of a meal.
Pat the peppers dry with kitchen paper.
Heat {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} in a large frying pan over very high heat until the oil is shimmering and just starting to smoke. Add the peppers in a single layer. They should sizzle vigorously.

Cook without stirring for 1 to 2 minutes until the undersides blister and darken. Shake the pan and cook for another 1 to 2 minutes. The peppers should be blistered and slightly charred on all sides but still holding their shape.
Tip onto a plate. Scatter generously with sea salt flakes. Serve immediately while hot.

items.cause: Pan not hot enough, or too many peppers in the pan
items.fix: The pan must be very hot before the peppers go in. If you add too many at once the temperature drops and the peppers steam rather than fry. Cook in two batches if needed.

items.symptom: The peppers are soft and shrivelled rather than slightly firm
items.fix: The peppers need only 3 to 4 minutes total. Remove as soon as they are blistered on all sides; they will continue cooking slightly on the plate from residual heat.

content.text: Pimientos de Padrón appear on tapas menus everywhere in Spain and are one of the most reliably good things to order. The combination of salt, olive oil, and the char from the hot pan is so complete that nothing else is needed. At home they can be on the table before the guests have sat down. The unpredictability of the occasional hot one makes them sociable food.
